name: Brevard County
ami_source: HUD FY2026 income limits, Palm Bay-Melbourne-Titusville, FL MSA (low-income limit, 4-person household); 120% figure from the Florida Housing Finance Corporation 2026 Combined Income and Rent Limits (4-person household). Limits vary by household size; the program directs applicants to huduser.gov for income eligibility.
_ami_method_note: ami_80 is HUD's published FY2026 low-income (80%) limit, 4-person, effective 2026-05-01; MSFH materials point applicants to the HUD income limits page. ami_120 is the FHFC 2026 chart figure (2.4x the HUD very-low limit, the state's standard 120% method); MSFH publishes no dollar table of its own and has not pinned a moderate-income formula, so present 120% as the standard state figure, not a program guarantee.
_ami_editor_note: Verified 2026-06-11 against HUD FY2026 (effective 2026-05-01) and the FHFC 2026 chart. Re-verify when HUD posts FY2027 limits (spring 2027) or if MSFH publishes its own dollar table.

windstorm_context.premium_source: Florida Office of Insurance Regulation, Property Insurance Stability Report, January 1, 2026 (average homeowners premium including wind coverage, Market Intelligence Report data as of September 30, 2025)
windstorm_context.citizens_share_note: Citizens Property Insurance, the state-backed insurer of last resort, held 7,367 personal residential policies in Brevard as of April 30, 2026, out of 289,824 personal residential policies statewide.
windstorm_context._premium_editor_note: Re-verify when OIR publishes the July 2026 Stability Report. Citizens counts are dropping fast month over month; re-verify monthly.

wind_borne_debris_region: Portions of Brevard County fall within the Wind-Borne Debris Region under the Florida Building Code.

faq.q: How much does a wind mitigation inspection cost in Brevard?
faq.a: Typically around $100 to $150, and the report is valid for up to five years if nothing material changes on the home. Prices may run higher under the documentation requirements of the form revised in April 2026.

faq.q: Do I qualify for a My Safe Florida Home grant in Brevard?
faq.a: For the current cycle the program is limited to low- and moderate-income homeowners: household income at or below 80% of the county median qualifies as low income, and up to 120% as moderate income. The home must be a site-built, owner-occupied primary residence with homestead exemption, built before 2008, with insurance Coverage A of $700,000 or less. The exact dollar thresholds for Brevard are shown above.

faq.q: Did the wind mitigation form change?
faq.a: Yes. The OIR-B1-1802 form was revised effective April 1, 2026, the first major update in over a decade. Inspectors must now document credited features with photos, product approval numbers, and permits, and carriers are expected to begin applying credits based on the new form around July 2026.

faq.q: Is my older wind mitigation report still valid?
faq.a: Reports remain valid for up to five years. But if you have replaced your roof, windows, or doors since your last inspection, a new inspection under the 2026 form may capture credits your old report misses.

faq.q: Does opening protection have to cover every window and door?
faq.a: Yes. Opening protection is scored all-or-nothing: every opening needs qualifying protection (impact glass or rated exterior shutters) to earn the credit.

faq.q: How much can the discount actually save?
faq.a: Discounts apply to the windstorm portion of the premium, which is often 30 to 60% of the total bill in Florida. Among My Safe Florida Home grant recipients who reported outcomes, about 49% reported savings, averaging roughly $981 per year. Savings are not guaranteed and depend on the home, the features documented, and the carrier.
